lesson.title: Functions III: return vs print and early return
masteryOutcomes: Use return to pass results and apply early returns.

concept: Returning values from functions with return, how it differs from print, and using early returns to exit a function as soon as the answer is known
prerequisites: Defining a function with def, parameters, and indentation
Calling functions with arguments and tracing value flow

explanations.text: Imagine you ask a friend to do some math. If they shout the answer across the room, that's print — you hear it, but you can't grab it. If they write it on a note and hand it to you, that's return — now you hold the answer and can use it for the next thing. Functions that return hand you the note.

print() shows a value on the screen for a human to read — the program itself gets nothing back. return sends a value back to whoever called the function, so the caller can store it, do more math with it, or pass it along. For example, def double(x): return x * 2 lets you write result = double(5), and result now holds 10. If the function only printed 10, result would hold None, because a function with no return gives back None. return also ends the function immediately.

Every function call in Python evaluates to a value. return specifies that value and immediately terminates the function — no code after an executed return runs. A function that falls off the end (or uses a bare return) evaluates to None. print() is unrelated to this: it's just a function that writes text to the output panel and itself returns None. Because return exits instantly, you can use early returns: check a special case first, return right away if it applies, and let the rest of the function handle the normal case. This flattens nested if/else logic. For example: def safe_divide(a, b): if b == 0: return None (early exit), then return a / b for the normal path.

examples:
def double(x):
    return x * 2

result = double(5)
print(result)  # 10

def shout(word):
    print(word.upper())

value = shout("hi")
print(value)  # HI, then None — printing gave nothing back

hintLadder.text: Ask yourself: does the caller need to USE this value later, or just SEE it? If the caller needs it, print won't help — which keyword hands the value back?
Inside the function, replace the print(...) line with return followed by the value itself — no parentheses needed after return.
For the special case, check it first with an if, and put a return right inside that if — remember return exits the function immediately, so the rest only runs in the normal case.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes print and return are the same because both 'show the answer' when testing in the editor
misconceptions.correction: They look similar only because you often print the returned value. The test: try result = my_function(5) then print(result). If the function used return, result holds the value; if it only printed, result is None and the value is gone.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes return x * 2 and then more lines after it, expecting them to run
misconceptions.correction: return ends the function instantly. Any code after an executed return never runs. If you need something to happen, put it before the return.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes return print(x) thinking it returns the printed value
misconceptions.correction: print() itself returns None, so return print(x) shows x on screen but hands None back to the caller. Write return x to hand back the value, and let the caller decide whether to print it.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks a function without return gives back the last value it computed
misconceptions.correction: Python doesn't guess. A function with no return (or a bare return) always gives back None. If you want a value out, you must return it explicitly.

misconceptions.trigger: Wraps everything in nested if/else instead of using an early return
misconceptions.correction: When a return sits inside an if, everything after that if only runs when the condition was false — so you don't need an else. Handle the special case with an early return, then write the normal case flat underneath.

checks.prompt: Write a function double(x) that RETURNS x times 2, then write result = double(7) and print(result). Run it — you should see 14.
acceptanceCriteria: Defines double with def and one parameter
Uses return x * 2 (not print) inside the function
Calls double(7), stores the result in a variable, and prints it
checks.passFeedback: That's the real thing — double handed 14 back, you caught it in result, and you chose to print it. That's return doing its job.
checks.failFeedback: Check two spots: the function body should say return x * 2, and outside the function you need result = double(7) then print(result). If you see None, the function is printing instead of returning.

checks.prompt: Write a function first_letter(word) that returns "empty" if word is "" (use an early return), and otherwise returns the first character word[0]. Test it with print(first_letter("python")) and print(first_letter("")).
acceptanceCriteria: Checks the empty-string case first with an if
Returns "empty" inside that if, before the normal path
Returns word[0] on the normal path without needing an else
Running it outputs p then empty
checks.passFeedback: Beautiful — the early return handled the edge case in one line and the normal case reads clean underneath. That's a professional pattern.
checks.failFeedback: Structure it as: if word == "": return "empty" first, then return word[0] on the next line at function-body level. Run it and check you see p then empty.

practice.prompt: Write a function grade(score) that returns "invalid" if score is less than 0 or greater than 100 (early return), returns "pass" if score is 50 or above, and returns "fail" otherwise. Print the results of grade(75), grade(30), and grade(150).
practice.starterCode:
def grade(score):
    # 1) early return for invalid scores
    # 2) return "pass" or "fail"


print(grade(75))
print(grade(30))
print(grade(150))
acceptanceCriteria: Handles the invalid range with an early return before the pass/fail logic
Uses return (not print) for all three results inside the function
Runs without errors and outputs pass, fail, invalid in that order

practice.prompt: A friend wrote a function that prints a total instead of returning it, and now total = calc() gives them None. In your own words, explain what went wrong and how to fix it.
rubric: Explains that print only displays the value; it does not send it back to the caller
States that a function without return gives back None
Says the fix is to use return total inside the function (and print at the call site if they want to see it)

practice.prompt:
What does this print?

def check(n):
    if n > 10:
        return "big"
    return "small"
    return "never"

print(check(20))
practice.explanation: n is 20, so the condition is true and return "big" runs, ending the function immediately. The later returns never execute — return exits on the spot.

pedagogyRules: Always use the 'catch the value' test to settle print-vs-return debates: have the student assign the call to a variable and print the variable — None means no return.
When the student writes return print(x) or code after a return, point at the exact line and ask what runs after return executes; don't rewrite the whole function for them.
If the student says they already know return, verify lightly with the early-return check (first_letter) instead of re-teaching the basics, then advance.
Teach early return as a readability win, not a rule: show the flat version next to the nested if/else version and ask which they'd rather read.
Keep the student running code in the editor after every change — the None appearing in the output is more convincing than any explanation.
If the student asks 'why not just print everything?', give the one-line answer (you can't reuse a printed value) and immediately set the double() task so they feel it.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Validated wrong work — student's function prints instead of returns but the output looks right
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Do not confirm success from screen output alone. Have them run result = f(...) then print(result); only confirm if result holds the value rather than None.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — student is asked to combine early return with pass/fail logic before grasping that return exits the function
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Step back one rung: give a two-line function with a single early return, have them trace which line runs for two different inputs, then return to the full task.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor misdiagnoses an error — e.g., blames the return when the real issue is 'return' outside function due to indentation
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Read the actual error from the Output panel. SyntaxError: 'return' outside function means indentation put the return at top level — name that exact cause and point at the indentation, not the return logic.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student frustrated: 'print worked fine for two weeks, why is everything None now?'
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Validate the frustration in one sentence, then show the payoff concretely: double(double(3)) works only because return chains values. Give them that one line to run before any more theory.

chunks.text: print() displays a value on the screen for a human to read; the program gets nothing back from it. return sends a value back to the caller so the program can store it, compute with it, or pass it to another function. Example: def double(x): return x * 2 — then result = double(5) makes result hold 10. If double only printed, result would hold None.

A function with no return statement (or a bare return) gives back None. That's why total = f() prints None when f only prints internally. The reliable test: assign the call to a variable and print the variable — if you see None, the function isn't returning. Also note return print(x) returns None, because print() itself returns None.

return ends the function immediately — no code after an executed return runs. In def check(n): if n > 10: return "big" then return "small", calling check(20) returns "big" and never reaches the second return; check(3) skips the if and returns "small".

An early return handles a special case first and exits, letting the normal case read flat with no else needed. Worked example: def safe_divide(a, b): if b == 0: return "cannot divide by zero" (early exit), then return a / b. Calling safe_divide(10, 2) gives 5.0; safe_divide(10, 0) gives the message because the early return fired before the division could error.

Returned values chain: because double(3) IS the value 6, you can write double(double(3)) to get 12, or total = double(3) + double(4) to get 14. Printed values can't do this — once printed, the value is gone from the program. This composability is the main practical reason to return rather than print.

celebration.headline: You now control what your functions give back.
celebration.recap: You separated showing a value (print) from handing it back (return), caught returned values in variables, and used early returns to exit cleanly on special cases.
celebration.unlocked: Next: functions that call other functions — chaining returned values into real programs.

summaryBullets: You can now tell whether a function prints or returns by catching its result in a variable and checking for None.
You can now write functions that return values so the caller can store, reuse, and chain them.
You can now use an early return to handle a special case and exit a function immediately.
You can now explain why code after an executed return never runs, and predict a function's output from its return paths.
openingLine: {name}, here's a question that trips up almost everyone: if a function shows you the right answer on screen, does your program actually have that answer? Surprisingly, often not — and today you'll learn the keyword that fixes it. Want to see the trap first?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you traced how values flow into a function through its parameters — today you'll master how values flow back out, using return instead of print.

dialogueExemplars.title: Wrong but confident: 'print and return are the same'

turns.text: I already get this — print and return both give you the answer, they're basically the same thing.

A lot of people think that, and I get why — on screen they look identical. Let's test it. Type this in the editor and press Run: def f(): print(10), then result = f(), then print(result). What does result show?
Huh. It printed 10 but then it printed None. Why?
That None is the giveaway — print showed 10 to you, but handed nothing back to the program, so result caught None. Change print(10) to return 10 and run it again. What do you see now?
Now it prints 10 twice. Okay, so return actually gives the value back.
Exactly — print is for your eyes, return is for your program. You just discovered that yourself with one experiment. Ready to make return do something print never could?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need return?

turns.text: Honestly, print has worked fine for two weeks. Why do I need return at all?

Fair challenge! Here's the real-world answer: a shopping app computes your cart total in one function, then a tax function needs that number, then a checkout function needs the result of that. Printed values are gone the moment they hit the screen — returned values can be passed along. Want to feel the difference in one line?
Write def double(x): return x * 2 in the editor, then run print(double(double(3))). What number comes out, and could a print-only version ever do that?
12! And no — the printed value couldn't feed into the second call.
That's the whole reason return exists: values that chain. Now let's add your first early return to that function — shall we?
I'm lost. Why would I return early? Doesn't the function need to finish?

Good instinct to ask — here's the key: return IS how a function finishes, and it can finish the moment it knows the answer. Think of a bouncer checking IDs: if you're under 18, the answer is 'no' immediately — no need to check the dress code. Does that picture help?
Kind of, but I don't see what it looks like in code.
Let's make it tiny. Type this and run it: def safe_divide(a, b): if b == 0: return "no dividing by zero", then on the next line return a / b. Try print(safe_divide(10, 0)) — which return fires?
The first one — it never even gets to the division!
Exactly, the early return exited before the crash could happen. That's the whole trick: handle the weird case first, exit, and the normal case reads clean below. Want to try writing one yourself?
